title: Basic Nursing Care — Safety, Comfort & Physical Needs (Set 2)
subtitle: Skin, nutrition/hydration, elimination, mobility, oxygen, glucose & emergency basics
scenario: Meeting clients' basic physical needs safely: skin integrity, nutrition and hydration, elimination, mobility, oxygen, blood glucose, and recognizing changes to report.

steps.q: To prevent pressure injuries in a client who is immobile, the technician should reposition the client at least every:

opts.text: 2 hours
opts.rat: Repositioning at least every 2 hours relieves pressure over bony prominences and helps prevent pressure injuries.

opts.text: 8 hours
opts.rat: Eight hours is far too long; tissue damage can begin within a couple of hours.

opts.text: 12 hours
opts.rat: This interval greatly increases pressure-injury risk.

opts.text: 24 hours
opts.rat: Once daily repositioning is grossly inadequate.

steps.q: A client is eating in the dayroom, suddenly clutches the throat, and cannot speak, cough, or breathe. The technician should:

opts.text: Offer the client a glass of water
opts.rat: Giving fluids to a choking person can worsen airway obstruction.

opts.text: Perform abdominal thrusts (Heimlich maneuver)
opts.rat: A conscious, choking adult with a complete obstruction (no air movement) needs immediate abdominal thrusts.

opts.text: Wait to see if the client can clear it alone
opts.rat: Complete obstruction is a life threat; waiting risks death.

opts.text: Lay the client flat and elevate the legs
opts.rat: This does not relieve an airway obstruction.

steps.q: A client on insulin becomes shaky, diaphoretic, and confused. The technician suspects hypoglycemia and, if the client is awake and able to swallow, should:

opts.text: Withhold all food until the next meal
opts.rat: Withholding carbohydrate worsens hypoglycemia and endangers the client.

opts.text: Encourage the client to exercise
opts.rat: Activity lowers glucose further, worsening hypoglycemia.

opts.text: Give a fast-acting carbohydrate such as juice, then notify the nurse
opts.rat: Awake, hypoglycemic clients who can swallow should receive a fast-acting carbohydrate, followed by prompt reporting and reassessment.

opts.text: Give a large dose of the client's insulin
opts.rat: Insulin further lowers glucose and could be fatal.

steps.q: Which findings BEST indicate that a client may be dehydrated?

opts.text: Moist mucous membranes and large urine output
opts.rat: These are signs of adequate hydration.

opts.text: Bounding pulse and elevated blood pressure
opts.rat: Dehydration more often produces a weak, rapid pulse and low blood pressure.

opts.text: Weight gain and edema
opts.rat: These suggest fluid overload, not dehydration.

opts.text: Dry mucous membranes, decreased urine output, and poor skin turgor
opts.rat: Dry mucous membranes, low urine output, and poor skin turgor are classic signs of dehydration.

steps.q: When recording intake and output (I&O), which is counted as OUTPUT?

opts.text: Urine and emesis (vomit)
opts.rat: Output includes measurable fluid losses such as urine, emesis, and drainage.

opts.text: Gelatin and ice chips consumed
opts.rat: Gelatin and ice chips are counted as intake.

opts.text: Water taken with medications
opts.rat: Fluids taken by mouth are intake.

opts.text: Soup at lunch
opts.rat: Liquid foods count as intake.

steps.q: Using proper body mechanics when helping to transfer a client, the technician should:

opts.text: Bend at the waist and keep the knees straight
opts.rat: Bending at the waist strains the back and risks injury.

opts.text: Bend the knees, keep the back straight, and hold the client close
opts.rat: Bending the knees, keeping the back straight, using the legs, and keeping the load close protect the back during transfers.

opts.text: Twist the spine while lifting to save time
opts.rat: Twisting while lifting is a common cause of back injury.

opts.text: Hold the client as far from the body as possible
opts.rat: Holding the load away from the body increases strain and instability.

steps.q: A client is receiving oxygen therapy. An important safety measure is to:

opts.text: Allow smoking near the oxygen source
opts.rat: Oxygen supports combustion; smoking or open flames near it can cause fire.

opts.text: Store the tank lying loose on the floor
opts.rat: Tanks must be secured to prevent falls/damage; this is a safety hazard.

opts.text: Keep the oxygen away from open flames and post 'no smoking' signage
opts.rat: Because oxygen supports combustion, ignition sources and smoking must be kept away and warning signage posted.

opts.text: Use petroleum jelly around the nares
opts.rat: Petroleum products near oxygen are flammable; water-based products are used.

steps.q: A pulse oximeter reads 84%, but the client is talking comfortably with warm, pink skin. The technician should FIRST:

opts.text: Document the value and take no further action
opts.rat: An abnormal reading must be verified and acted on, not simply recorded.

opts.text: Immediately remove all clothing
opts.rat: This does not address the measurement or the client's status.

opts.text: Tell the client to hold their breath
opts.rat: This would worsen oxygenation and is inappropriate.

opts.text: Check probe placement and perfusion, then reassess and report
opts.rat: Poor probe placement, movement, cold digits, or nail polish can cause false low readings; confirm the reading, then report a true low value.

steps.q: To help prevent constipation in a client, the technician should encourage:

opts.text: Adequate fluids, dietary fiber, and physical activity as tolerated
opts.rat: Fluids, fiber, and activity support normal bowel elimination and help prevent constipation.

opts.text: Decreased fluids and bed rest
opts.rat: Low fluids and inactivity promote constipation.

opts.text: A low-fiber diet
opts.rat: Low fiber contributes to constipation.

opts.text: Routinely ignoring the urge to defecate
opts.rat: Suppressing the urge worsens constipation.

steps.q: To reduce aspiration risk, a client who has just finished eating should be kept:

opts.text: Lying flat immediately
opts.rat: Lying flat after meals increases reflux and aspiration risk.

opts.text: Upright (sitting) for about 30–60 minutes after the meal
opts.rat: Remaining upright after eating reduces reflux and aspiration risk.

opts.text: In the Trendelenburg (head-down) position
opts.rat: Head-down positioning increases aspiration risk.

opts.text: Turned face-down
opts.rat: Prone positioning after meals is unsafe and inappropriate.

steps.q: The MOST reliable indicator of a client's pain is:

opts.text: The technician's personal judgment of how much it 'should' hurt
opts.rat: Staff assumptions are unreliable and can lead to undertreatment.

opts.text: Whether the diagnosis 'usually' causes pain
opts.rat: Diagnosis-based assumptions do not replace the client's report.

opts.text: The client's own self-report of pain
opts.rat: Pain is subjective; the client's self-report is the most reliable indicator when they can communicate.

opts.text: The presence of visible bleeding only
opts.rat: Pain can be severe without visible injury; absence of bleeding does not rule it out.

steps.q: During a skin check, the technician notes an area of redness over the sacrum that does not turn white (blanch) when pressed. The technician should:

opts.text: Massage the reddened area vigorously
opts.rat: Vigorous massage of compromised skin can cause further tissue damage.

opts.text: Ignore it since the skin is intact
opts.rat: Intact but non-blanching redness is an early pressure injury requiring action.

opts.text: Apply a heating pad to the area
opts.rat: Heat is not indicated and could worsen tissue injury.

opts.text: Report and document it and relieve pressure on the area
opts.rat: Non-blanching redness is an early (stage 1) pressure injury; it must be reported/documented and pressure relieved.

steps.q: A client uses a cane for balance after a left-leg weakness. The technician teaches the client to hold the cane:

opts.text: On the strong (right) side, moving cane and weak leg together
opts.rat: The cane is held on the stronger side; the cane and the weaker leg advance together for support and stability.

opts.text: On the weak (left) side
opts.rat: Holding the cane on the weak side does not provide effective support.

opts.text: In whichever hand is dominant regardless of weakness
opts.rat: Cane placement is based on the affected side, not hand dominance.

opts.text: Above the head for balance
opts.rat: This is unsafe and provides no support.

steps.q: For a client with an indwelling urinary catheter, the drainage bag should always be positioned:

opts.text: Above the level of the bladder
opts.rat: Raising the bag above the bladder allows urine to flow back, increasing infection risk.

opts.text: Below the level of the bladder without touching the floor
opts.rat: Keeping the bag below the bladder promotes gravity drainage and prevents backflow; it should not rest on the floor (contamination).

opts.text: Clipped to the bed rail at chest height
opts.rat: This places the bag above the bladder, risking backflow.

opts.text: Resting on the client's abdomen
opts.rat: This is above the bladder and promotes backflow.

steps.q: Which measure BEST promotes sleep for a client with insomnia on the unit?

opts.text: Offer coffee in the evening
opts.rat: Caffeine is a stimulant that impairs sleep.

opts.text: Encourage a long daytime nap
opts.rat: Long daytime naps can worsen nighttime insomnia.

opts.text: Maintain a consistent bedtime routine and reduce evening noise/light/caffeine
opts.rat: Good sleep hygiene—consistent routine, reduced stimulation and caffeine—supports natural sleep.

opts.text: Keep the lights and television on all night
opts.rat: Light and noise disrupt sleep.

steps.q: A client refuses to eat and has lost weight. The technician's BEST action is to:

opts.text: Force the client to eat
opts.rat: Forcing food violates rights and can cause aspiration or injury.

opts.text: Ignore the weight loss
opts.rat: Unaddressed weight loss can lead to serious complications.

opts.text: Remove all food and fluids
opts.rat: Withholding nutrition and hydration is harmful.

opts.text: Document intake/weight, offer preferred foods, and report the poor intake to the nurse
opts.rat: Monitoring and documenting intake/weight, offering acceptable foods, and reporting decline allow the team to intervene appropriately.

steps.q: The BEST way to reduce the need for physical restraints is to:

opts.text: Use frequent observation, meet needs proactively, and provide activity/orientation
opts.rat: Least-restrictive care uses monitoring, meeting needs (toileting, comfort, activity), and orientation to reduce agitation and restraint use.

opts.text: Apply restraints early on all confused clients
opts.rat: Routine restraint is restrictive and increases injury risk; it is not preventive care.

opts.text: Isolate clients in locked rooms
opts.rat: Seclusion is itself a restrictive intervention, not a restraint-reduction strategy.

opts.text: Sedate all restless clients
opts.rat: Chemical restraint for convenience is inappropriate and regulated.

steps.q: When a client has a fever, an appropriate basic nursing measure is to:

opts.text: Cover the client with heavy blankets to sweat it out
opts.rat: Excessive covering traps heat and can raise temperature further.

opts.text: Encourage fluids, provide light clothing/covers, and report the fever
opts.rat: Promoting fluids, avoiding overheating, and reporting the fever support the client and allow ordered treatment.

opts.text: Restrict all fluids
opts.rat: Fever increases fluid needs; restricting fluids risks dehydration.

opts.text: Give aspirin without an order
opts.rat: Administering medication without an order is outside protocol and unsafe.

steps.q: The technician assists a client who has been on bed rest to stand, and the client reports dizziness. The technician should FIRST:

opts.text: Encourage the client to walk quickly to 'shake it off'
opts.rat: Walking while dizzy risks a fall.

opts.text: Leave the client standing alone to adjust
opts.rat: Leaving a dizzy client unsupported risks injury.

opts.text: Help the client sit or lie back down and rise slowly, then reassess
opts.rat: Dizziness on standing suggests orthostatic changes; returning the client to a safe position and changing position slowly prevents falls.

opts.text: Give caffeine to raise the blood pressure
opts.rat: Caffeine is not an appropriate intervention for positional dizziness.

steps.q: Which change should the technician report to the nurse IMMEDIATELY?

opts.text: A client who slept well and ate breakfast
opts.rat: This is a normal, reassuring finding.

opts.text: A client requesting a second helping at lunch
opts.rat: This is not an urgent clinical concern.

opts.text: A client watching television in the dayroom
opts.rat: This is normal activity, not a change to escalate.

opts.text: New confusion, chest pain, or difficulty breathing
opts.rat: Acute changes such as new confusion, chest pain, or respiratory distress are potential emergencies requiring immediate reporting.
